title: Solvren Documentation
description: Learn how to safely manage revenue-impacting operational changes.
content: Solvren Documentation Solvren helps organizations safely manage operational changes that impact revenue systems. The platform provides structured workflows for: • evaluating operational risk • coordinating approvals • documenting safeguards • maintaining audit history Start Here If you're new to Solvren, begin with these guides. | Guide | Description | | | | | Get Started | Learn how to set up your organization | | Create Your First Change | Walk through a full change workflow | | User Guide | Understand how the platform works | Core Concepts Understanding these concepts will help you use Solvren effectively. | Concept | Description | | | | | What is a Change | The core unit of governance | | Domains | Functional areas affected by changes | | Systems | Platforms where changes occur | | Evidence | Safeguards required for operational readiness | Operating the Platform Learn how to manage operational changes. | Guide | Description | | | | | Change Lifecycle | How changes progress through the system | | Approvals Workflow | How reviewers evaluate changes | | Governance Best Practices | Recommended operational policies | Administration Administrators configure governance policies. | Admin Guide | Description | | | | | Organization Settings | Configure organization wide settings | | User Management | Manage users and roles | | Approval Role Mapping | Configure approval routing | | Evidence Enforcement | Define required safeguards | Architecture These pages explain how the system works internally. | Architecture Guide | Description | | | | | System Overview | Platform architecture | | AI Risk Engine | Risk analysis model | | Coordination Autopilot | Automated governance planning | Operational Playbooks These guides help teams operate Solvren effectively. | Playbook | Description | | | | | Governance Playbook | Recommended governance structure | | Change Review Runbook | Step by step review process | Examples Real examples demonstrate how governance workflows operate. | Example | Description | | | | | Pricing Change | Pricing configuration governance | | Billing Change | Managing billing system updates | Releases Track product improvements and updates. | Page | Description | | | | | Release Notes | Major feature updates | | Changelog | Detailed version history |

title: Approval Mappings
description: Define how systems, domains, and change types map to the right reviewers automatically.
content: What approval mappings do Approval mappings power Coordination Autopilot. They answer: who should review Finance changes who should review Stripe changes who should review Pricing changes Common mapping examples Domain: Finance → Finance Reviewer System: Stripe → Billing Owner Change Type: Pricing → Revenue Leadership Why this matters Without mappings, coordination remains manual. With mappings, the system can automatically suggest the correct reviewers when a change is created or updated. Recommended mapping strategy Start with: 1. domain mappings 2. critical system mappings 3. high risk change type mappings Then expand after the first few live workflows. Validation tips Before relying on mappings in production like workflows, confirm: each mapped role has members the members actually have domain review permission where required your most common change types resolve to the expected reviewers

title: Approval Role Mapping
description: Configure how approvals are routed for operational changes.
content: Overview Approval Role Mapping determines which stakeholders must review a change before it can be approved. These mappings allow organizations to enforce governance policies consistently. Approval requirements are based on: • change domain • systems involved • change type How Approval Mapping Works Solvren uses approval roles instead of individual users. Roles represent organizational responsibilities such as: • Finance • Engineering • Product • Revenue Operations Users are assigned to these roles within the user management settings. Example Approval Mapping Organizations typically configure mappings similar to the following: | Domain | Required Approvers | | | | | Pricing | Finance + Product | | Billing | Engineering + Finance | | CRM | Revenue Operations | When a change is created, the system automatically identifies the required reviewers. Configuring Approval Mappings Administrators can configure mappings by: 1. Navigating to Admin → Approval Settings 2. Selecting the domain or change type 3. Assigning required roles Once configured, these rules automatically apply to all changes. Why Approval Mapping Matters Approval mappings ensure that governance policies are applied consistently. Without structured routing, critical stakeholders may be excluded from important operational decisions. Automated routing helps ensure that: • appropriate teams review changes • approvals are consistent across the organization • governance policies are enforced Best Practices Recommended practices include: • requiring finance approval for pricing changes • requiring engineering review for system configuration changes • including revenue operations for CRM changes These policies help reduce operational risk.

title: Approval Roles
description: Define reusable governance responsibility groups assignable through mappings and coordination plans.
content: What approval roles are Approval roles are reusable groups for governance responsibility. Examples: Finance Reviewer, Billing Owner, Security Reviewer, Revenue Leadership, Data Reviewer. Why approval roles matter Roles make governance more stable than assigning individuals. Define that Stripe changes go to Billing Owner, then manage Billing Owner membership separately. How approval roles are used Approval roles feed into approval mappings, Coordination Autopilot, reviewer suggestions, and coverage checks. Best practice Use approval roles for responsibilities that outlive any single individual.

title: Audit Logs
description: Track historical activity and governance decisions.
content: Overview Audit Logs record all significant actions performed within Solvren. These logs provide a complete history of governance activity. Audit logs support: • operational accountability • compliance requirements • incident investigations Administrators can review these records to understand how decisions were made. Logged Events Solvren records events such as: • change creation • evidence updates • approval decisions • comment activity • configuration changes Each log entry includes: • timestamp • user identity • action performed • associated change Reviewing Audit Logs Administrators can review logs by: 1. Navigating to Admin → Audit Logs 2. Filtering events by user, change, or date 3. Reviewing historical actions This allows administrators to investigate operational activity. Why Audit Logs Matter Audit logs provide transparency into governance workflows. Organizations use audit logs to: • review operational decisions • investigate incidents • verify compliance policies These records ensure that operational changes remain traceable. Retention Policies Organizations may configure retention policies for audit logs depending on internal governance requirements. Some organizations retain audit records for several years for compliance purposes.

title: Domain Permissions
description: Control which users can view and review changes in specific governance domains.
content: What domain permissions control Domain permissions refine access beyond base org roles. They answer: who can see Finance changes who can review Security changes who should be excluded from restricted domains Typical domains Examples: Finance Security Data Operations Legal Common permission model A user may have: view access review access This allows some users to observe a domain while limiting approval authority to a smaller set. Why domain permissions matter They protect against overexposure and keep reviewers aligned with real responsibilities. Approval mappings and domain permissions should align. If the system suggests a reviewer for a domain but that user lacks review permission for that domain, the coordination plan should surface a coverage problem.

title: Evidence Enforcement
description: Configure required safeguards for operational changes.
content: Overview Evidence Enforcement allows administrators to require operational safeguards before changes can be submitted for review. These safeguards help ensure that teams have prepared for potential operational risks. Organizations can configure evidence requirements based on: • change domain • systems involved • change type Enforcing evidence helps prevent operational failures caused by insufficient preparation. Evidence Types Common evidence requirements include: Rollback Plan Describes how the change can be reversed if problems occur. Example rollback actions: • restore previous pricing configuration • revert billing rules • disable routing changes Testing Documentation Confirms that the change was validated before deployment. Examples include: • billing simulations • pricing rule validation • invoice generation tests Monitoring Plan Describes how teams will detect issues after the change is deployed. Examples include: • revenue anomaly alerts • billing error dashboards • monitoring system integrations Revenue Validation Ensures that the change does not introduce financial inaccuracies. Examples include: • sample invoice verification • reconciliation checks • financial reporting validation Configuring Evidence Requirements Administrators can configure required evidence by: 1. Navigating to Admin → Evidence Policies 2. Selecting the domain or system 3. Defining required evidence types These requirements are automatically enforced when users create changes. Why Evidence Enforcement Matters Operational incidents often occur when safeguards are missing. Evidence enforcement ensures that teams have: • tested the change • prepared recovery procedures • implemented monitoring This significantly reduces operational risk. Best Practices Organizations should require stronger safeguards for high risk domains such as: • billing configuration • pricing updates • revenue recognition Lower risk changes may require fewer safeguards.

title: Jira Integration
description: Connect Solvren to Jira Cloud for change creation and status sync.
content: Overview The Jira integration connects Solvren governance workflows to engineering work tracked in Jira Cloud. Features: • Create Solvren changes from Jira issues • Link changes to Jira issues • Sync status between Jira and Solvren • Display governance status in Jira Supported Environments • Jira Cloud — Supported • Jira Server / Data Center — Not supported in V1 Setup 1. Create an Atlassian Developer App 1. Go to 2. Create an app → OAuth 2.0 (3LO) 3. Add callback URL: 4. Configure scopes: , , , , , 2. Environment Variables Set in (ATLASSIAN takes precedence over JIRA ): 3. Connect from Org Settings 1. Go to Org settings → Jira integration (or Settings → Connect Jira / Configure Jira ) 2. Click Connect Jira 3. Authorize the app in Atlassian 4. After connection, configure projects, issue types, status mappings, and feature toggles Create Change from Jira Use the API to create a Solvren change from a Jira issue: Payload: Response: Webhook Setup To sync Jira status changes to Solvren, register a webhook in Jira: 1. In Jira: Settings → System → WebHooks 2. Create webhook with URL: 3. Events: Issue updated , Issue deleted 4. Copy the Webhook ID and ensure is sent (Jira Cloud sends this by default) Status Mapping Jira statuses map to Solvren statuses: | Jira Status | Solvren Status | | | | | To Do | DRAFT | | In Progress | DRAFT | | Ready for Review | READY | | In Review | IN REVIEW | | Done | APPROVED | | Rejected | REJECTED | Admins can customize mappings in . Phase 2 Features Webhook registration When Webhook sync is enabled in config and you save, the integration registers a Jira webhook for issue updated/deleted and comment created. Webhooks expire after 30 days; refresh via Jira admin or reconnect. Governance status in Jira When Issue property sync is enabled, Solvren writes to linked Jira issues (status, risk score, approval progress, link to change). Comment sync When Comment sync is enabled, governance events (change created, approval requested/completed/rejected, change approved) are posted as Jira comments with a prefix. Token refresh Access tokens are refreshed automatically before expiry (10 minute buffer). Failed refresh marks the integration as error; reconnect from org settings. Retry logic Failed webhook events are retried with exponential backoff. Use Retry failures in the Jira config page to queue pending failures for immediate retry. Cron jobs run (every 2 min) and (every 5 min). Troubleshooting Jira not configured — Ensure and are set. OAuth callback fails — Verify matches the URL registered in Atlassian. Webhook not processing — Ensure header is present. Check for retry queue. Token expired — Reconnect Jira from org settings. Ensure scope and refresh token are stored.

title: Notification Configuration
description: Configure how users receive alerts about change activity.
content: Overview Notification Configuration controls how users receive alerts about changes that require their attention. Notifications ensure that governance workflows progress efficiently. Administrators can configure: • notification channels • alert rules • delivery preferences Notification Channels Solvren supports several notification channels. Email Notifications Email alerts notify users when: • approvals are requested • evidence is missing • reviewers leave comments • changes become overdue Email notifications ensure that important events are not missed. Slack Notifications Slack notifications deliver alerts directly to team communication channels. Common Slack alerts include: • approval requests • completed approvals • change submissions Slack integration helps teams coordinate quickly. In App Notifications In app alerts appear within the Solvren interface. These alerts provide immediate visibility when users log in. Configuring Notifications Administrators can configure notification settings by: 1. Navigating to Admin → Notifications 2. Selecting the notification channel 3. Configuring delivery preferences These settings apply to all users within the organization. Recommended Configuration Organizations typically configure notifications to ensure that: • reviewers receive approval alerts immediately • operational teams receive deployment alerts • overdue approvals trigger reminders Proper notification settings help maintain efficient governance workflows.

title: Organization Settings
description: Configure organization-wide settings in Solvren.
content: Overview Organization Settings control how Solvren operates within your company. These settings define: • organizational metadata • governance defaults • security boundaries • integration configuration Only administrators or organization owners can modify these settings. Organization Information Administrators can configure basic organization metadata. Typical settings include: • organization name • default timezone • primary contact email This information is used for notifications and audit records. Governance Defaults Governance defaults determine how changes are evaluated across the organization. Examples include: • default approval requirements • required evidence types • notification policies These defaults provide a baseline level of operational oversight. Integration Configuration Organizations may integrate Solvren with external tools. Common integrations include: • Slack for notifications • email systems for alerts • internal operational platforms Administrators configure integration credentials and permissions within this section. Security Settings Security configuration helps protect sensitive operational data. Administrators may configure: • authentication policies • session timeouts • role based permissions These controls ensure that only authorized users can access governance data. Best Practices Organizations should review settings periodically to ensure they reflect current operational policies. Recommended practices include: • updating approval policies when teams change • reviewing notification settings regularly • auditing integration access Proper configuration ensures that governance workflows remain effective.

title: Organization Setup
description: Configure Solvren for operational use with users, systems, roles, mappings, and defaults.
content: Overview A strong organization setup ensures that Coordination Autopilot, approvals, evidence, and visibility all work correctly from the start. What to configure first Confirm organization name, timezone, notification settings, and review SLA defaults. Add owners, admins, submitters, reviewers, and viewers. Add systems like Stripe, Chargebee, HubSpot, Salesforce, and NetSuite. Define who owns Finance, Security, Billing, Data, and other governance areas. Recommended initial roles Typical roles include Owner, Admin, Submitter, Reviewer, and Viewer. Recommended initial approval roles Examples: Finance Reviewer, Security Reviewer, Billing Owner, Revenue Leadership, Data Reviewer.

title: User Management
description: Manage users and access within your organization.
content: Overview User Management allows administrators to control who can access Solvren and what actions they can perform. Proper user configuration ensures that governance workflows involve the correct stakeholders. Inviting Users Administrators can invite users to the organization. To invite a user: 1. Navigate to Admin → Users 2. Click Invite User 3. Enter the user's email address 4. Assign a role The user will receive an invitation email with instructions for joining the organization. User Roles Roles determine what actions users can perform within the system. Typical roles include: Owner Full system access. Owners can configure organization settings and manage governance policies. Admin Administrators manage system configuration. They can: • invite users • configure approvals • manage evidence policies Submitter Submitters can create and edit changes. They can also submit changes for review. Reviewer Reviewers evaluate and approve changes. They provide feedback and determine whether governance requirements have been satisfied. Managing User Access Administrators can update user roles at any time. They can also: • deactivate users • reassign roles • transfer ownership Removing unused accounts helps maintain system security. Best Practices Recommended practices include: • assigning reviewers from each operational domain • limiting administrative privileges • removing inactive accounts promptly These steps help ensure that governance workflows remain secure and efficient.

title: Users and Roles
description: Understand org roles, who should get them, and how they affect day-to-day work.
content: Org roles Solvren uses organization level roles to define what users can do. | Role | Responsibilities | | | | | Owner | Full configuration and governance control | | Admin | Manage users, settings, and governance setup | | Submitter | Create and submit changes | | Reviewer | Review and approve changes | | Viewer | Read only access where allowed | Assigning roles well Owner: executive sponsor. Admin: RevOps lead. Submitter: operators, engineers. Reviewer: finance, security stakeholders. Viewer: leadership, observers. Keep the number of owners small. Use Admin for operational configuration. Related concepts Org roles are different from approval roles, domain permissions, and explicit restricted access grants.

title: What is a Change?
description: The core unit of governance in Solvren.
content: Overview A Change represents an operational modification that could affect revenue systems. Solvren tracks these changes so organizations can evaluate risk, coordinate approvals, and document safeguards before implementation. Changes are the core unit of governance within the platform. Why Changes Matter Modern revenue operations involve many interconnected systems: • billing platforms • CRM systems • payment processors • marketing automation tools • financial reporting pipelines A modification to one system can unintentionally affect others. Tracking changes ensures that: • stakeholders are informed • dependencies are validated • safeguards are documented Examples of Changes Examples of revenue impacting changes include: Pricing Updates Modifying pricing tiers, discount rules, or promotional logic. Billing Configuration Updating billing cycles, invoice generation rules, or subscription logic. CRM Routing Changing lead routing rules or customer assignment logic. Revenue Recognition Adjusting accounting rules used for revenue reporting. Integration Changes Updating integrations between systems such as CRM and billing platforms. What Information a Change Contains Each change records structured information including: • change title and description • domain • systems involved • rollout strategy • operational dependencies • evidence and safeguards • approvals and reviewer comments This structured information enables the system to analyze risk. Change Lifecycle Each change progresses through a lifecycle: Draft → Ready → In Review → Approved → Archived This lifecycle ensures that governance requirements are satisfied before deployment. Key Principle Solvren focuses on high impact operational changes , not everyday tasks. If a modification could impact: • revenue generation • billing accuracy • customer pricing • financial reporting then it should be tracked as a change.

title: Coordination Plans
description: How Solvren ensures the right teams are involved in operational changes.
content: Overview Operational changes rarely affect a single team. Solvren generates Coordination Plans to ensure that all necessary stakeholders review and prepare for a change. These plans help organizations manage cross team dependencies. Why Coordination Matters Revenue systems are highly interconnected. A change to one system may impact several others. Examples: • pricing updates affecting billing systems • CRM routing changes affecting marketing automation • billing rule changes affecting financial reporting Coordination plans ensure that these dependencies are evaluated before deployment. Components of a Coordination Plan A coordination plan typically includes: • required approvals • evidence requirements • cross team coordination tasks These elements work together to ensure operational readiness. Approvals Approvals ensure that relevant stakeholders review the change. Examples: | Domain | Typical Approvers | | | | | Pricing | Finance + Product | | Billing | Engineering + Finance | | CRM | RevOps | Approval requirements are defined in the organization's governance settings. Evidence Requirements Evidence ensures safeguards are documented. Examples: • rollback plan • testing documentation • monitoring plan Evidence requirements help prevent operational failures. Coordination Tasks Some changes require explicit coordination steps. Examples: • notify finance of pricing changes • confirm CRM field mappings • validate integration dependencies These tasks help teams prepare for the change. Coordination Autopilot Solvren automatically generates coordination plans based on: • domain • systems involved • change type Users can apply these recommendations with a single action. This dramatically reduces the effort required to prepare a change for review. Key Principle Coordination plans ensure that operational changes are shared responsibilities , not isolated actions.

title: Domains
description: How Solvren categorizes operational changes.
content: Overview Domains categorize the functional area affected by a change. Domains help the platform determine: • required approvals • evidence requirements • operational risk Each organization may customize domains to reflect their operational structure. Common Domains Solvren commonly uses the following domains. Pricing Pricing changes affect how products or services are sold. Examples: • new pricing tiers • discount rules • promotional pricing logic Pricing changes often require finance or product review. Billing Billing changes affect how customers are invoiced and charged. Examples: • subscription billing configuration • invoice generation logic • billing cycle changes Billing modifications often require strong safeguards. Revenue Recognition Revenue recognition governs how revenue is recorded for accounting purposes. Examples: • deferred revenue schedules • recognition timing adjustments • accounting rule configuration Changes in this domain may require finance approval. Lead Routing Lead routing changes affect how new leads are assigned to sales teams. Examples: • territory assignments • routing rules • automation updates These changes typically involve revenue operations teams. Reporting Reporting changes affect dashboards or financial analytics pipelines. Examples: • data transformations • reporting definitions • financial metrics Although lower risk than billing changes, reporting updates may affect decision making. Why Domains Are Important Domains allow organizations to enforce governance policies such as: • domain specific approvals • evidence requirements • risk thresholds For example: | Domain | Typical Approvers | | | | | Pricing | Finance + Product | | Billing | Engineering + Finance | | CRM | RevOps | This ensures that the correct stakeholders review each change.

title: Evidence
description: Operational safeguards that ensure changes can be safely deployed.
content: Overview Evidence represents the operational safeguards required before implementing a change. These safeguards ensure that teams have: • tested the change • validated expected outcomes • prepared recovery procedures Evidence is a critical component of governance because it ensures that operational readiness is documented before deployment. Why Evidence Exists Operational failures often occur when teams implement changes without adequate safeguards. Common causes include: • insufficient testing • missing rollback procedures • incomplete monitoring Evidence ensures that teams proactively address these risks. Types of Evidence Organizations may require different types of evidence depending on the domain and system involved. Rollback Plan A rollback plan describes how to reverse a change if issues occur. Examples: • restore previous pricing configuration • revert CRM routing rules • disable a billing rule update Rollback procedures should be clear and executable. Testing Documentation Testing documentation verifies that the change behaves as expected. Examples: • billing simulations • pricing rule validation • sample invoice verification Testing reduces the likelihood of unexpected behavior. Monitoring Plan Monitoring ensures that teams can detect problems quickly after deployment. Examples: • revenue anomaly alerts • invoice failure monitoring • payment error dashboards Monitoring allows teams to respond rapidly if something goes wrong. Revenue Validation Revenue validation confirms that financial outcomes remain correct. Examples: • invoice verification • reconciliation checks • financial reporting validation These checks are particularly important for billing and pricing changes. Evidence Enforcement Organizations can configure required evidence types based on: • change domain • systems involved • change type For example: | Domain | Required Evidence | | | | | Billing | rollback plan | | Pricing | testing documentation | | Revenue recognition | financial reconciliation | Evidence requirements ensure that operational safeguards are consistently applied. Key Principle Evidence ensures that every change answers an important question: "If something goes wrong, how do we detect it and recover?"

title: Revenue Impact Reports
description: How Solvren evaluates the financial risk of operational changes.
content: Overview Revenue Impact Reports analyze the potential financial consequences of a change. These reports help reviewers understand: • potential failure modes • financial exposure • recommended safeguards The report is generated by the AI Risk Engine using structured change data. Why Revenue Impact Analysis Matters Revenue operations systems control critical financial processes. Even small configuration errors can lead to: • incorrect pricing • billing failures • duplicate charges • inaccurate financial reporting Revenue Impact Reports help teams evaluate these risks before deployment. Components of the Report A typical report includes several sections. Risk Score The risk score summarizes the potential financial impact of a change. Factors influencing risk include: • domain • systems involved • deployment scope • operational complexity Higher scores indicate greater potential risk. Failure Modes Failure modes describe ways the change could produce unintended outcomes. Examples include: • incorrect pricing rules • billing duplication • invoice generation failures • reporting discrepancies Understanding these scenarios helps reviewers evaluate risk. Safeguard Recommendations The system may recommend safeguards such as: • additional approvals • expanded monitoring • additional validation testing These safeguards help mitigate identified risks. Executive Summary The executive summary provides a concise explanation of the change and its risk profile. This section helps stakeholders quickly understand the implications of the change. How Reviewers Use the Report Reviewers evaluate the report when deciding whether to approve a change. They consider: • the severity of potential failure modes • whether safeguards are sufficient • whether additional validation is needed This structured evaluation improves governance consistency. Key Principle Revenue Impact Reports help organizations answer an essential question: "What financial risks could this change introduce?"

title: Systems
description: The external systems that operational changes interact with.
content: Overview Solvren tracks which systems are affected by a change. These systems represent the platforms where operational logic is implemented. Understanding system dependencies helps reviewers evaluate risk. Examples of Systems Organizations commonly integrate with multiple revenue systems. Examples include: Billing Platforms Systems responsible for customer billing. Examples: • Stripe • Chargebee • Recurly Changes to billing platforms can affect invoices, payments, and subscriptions. CRM Platforms Customer relationship management systems. Examples: • Salesforce • HubSpot CRM changes may affect lead routing, account ownership, or opportunity tracking. Financial Systems Systems responsible for accounting and revenue reporting. Examples: • NetSuite • QuickBooks • custom financial reporting pipelines Changes affecting financial systems may require finance approval. Marketing Platforms Marketing automation systems responsible for customer communication. Examples: • marketing automation tools • campaign platforms • email engagement systems Changes may affect lead capture or promotional campaigns. Internal Systems Many organizations operate internal services that support their revenue operations. Examples: • pricing microservices • internal billing APIs • custom reporting pipelines Changes to these systems may introduce hidden dependencies. Why Systems Matter Identifying systems involved in a change helps the platform: • recommend appropriate approvers • identify operational dependencies • estimate risk exposure For example, a change affecting both Stripe and Salesforce may require coordination between finance and revenue operations teams.

title: Example: Billing Logic Change
description: Governance workflow for billing system updates.
content: Scenario An organization needs to modify subscription billing rules. Systems involved: • billing platform • CRM system • financial reporting pipeline Governance Steps Approvals required: • Engineering • Finance Evidence required: • billing simulations • rollback procedure • monitoring plan Risks Identified Potential risks: • duplicate billing • invoice generation failures • revenue reporting discrepancies

title: Example: Pricing Configuration Change
description: Walkthrough of a real pricing change governed through Solvren.
content: Overview This example demonstrates how a pricing configuration change is managed through Solvren. Pricing changes are high impact because they directly affect customer billing and revenue generation. Scenario An organization wants to introduce a new pricing tier for a product. The change involves: • updating pricing logic in the billing platform • updating pricing documentation • verifying invoice generation Step 1 — Create the Change Example change configuration: | Field | Value | | | | | Title | Add new Pro pricing tier | | Domain | Pricing | | Systems | Stripe | | Change Type | Pricing configuration | Step 2 — Coordination Autopilot The system generates recommended governance steps. Suggested approvals: • Finance • Product Suggested evidence: • pricing rule validation tests • invoice simulation • rollback procedure Step 3 — Revenue Impact Report The system identifies potential risks: Failure modes: • incorrect pricing applied to existing customers • invoice calculation errors Risk score: Medium Recommended safeguards: • test invoice generation • verify billing tier mapping Step 4 — Submit for Review Approvers review: • change description • risk report • evidence checklist Finance verifies pricing logic before approval. Outcome After approvals are complete, the change is approved and recorded in the audit history. This example demonstrates how governance workflows reduce pricing configuration risk.

title: AI Risk Engine
description: How Solvren analyzes potential financial risk before a change is deployed.
content: Overview The AI Risk Engine analyzes operational changes to identify potential financial risk before deployment. By evaluating structured intake data and historical patterns, the system generates a Revenue Impact Report that helps reviewers understand the potential consequences of a change. This analysis allows organizations to proactively identify failure modes before they occur. Inputs The AI Risk Engine analyzes several categories of information. Change Metadata Information provided during intake: • change type • domain • systems involved • rollout method • customer impact • backfill requirements These signals provide context about how the change will affect revenue operations. Organizational Governance Rules Risk analysis considers the organization's configured policies, including: • required approvals • evidence requirements • domain specific safeguards These rules influence the final readiness evaluation. Operational Signals The system evaluates operational signals such as: • deployment scope • integration dependencies • data migration activity • financial system exposure Changes that modify billing or pricing systems typically receive higher risk scores. Risk Outputs The AI Risk Engine produces structured outputs including: Risk Score A numerical representation of potential risk. Higher scores indicate greater potential financial exposure. Failure Mode Analysis Identifies ways a change could cause unintended outcomes. Examples: • pricing misconfiguration • billing duplication • invoice generation failure • reporting discrepancies Recommended Safeguards The engine may recommend safeguards such as: • additional approvals • expanded monitoring • validation checks • rollback verification Purpose The AI Risk Engine helps organizations answer a critical question: "What could go wrong if we deploy this change?" By identifying risk early, teams can address issues before they impact revenue.

title: Architecture Overview
description: High-level architecture of Solvren.
content: Overview Solvren coordinates governance workflows across revenue systems. The platform connects change management workflows with risk analysis and operational safeguards. Core Architecture Solvren consists of several major components. Components Change Intake Collects structured metadata about operational changes. Coordination Engine Generates governance workflows including approvals and evidence requirements. Risk Analysis Engine Analyzes potential financial risk associated with a change. Approval Workflows Coordinate cross team review and decision making. Audit System Records historical decisions and governance activity.

title: Coordination Autopilot
description: How Solvren automatically recommends coordination tasks and approvals.
content: Overview Operational changes often require coordination across multiple teams. Coordination Autopilot analyzes change metadata and automatically recommends the governance steps required to safely implement a change. These recommendations ensure the right stakeholders are involved. What Coordination Autopilot Generates The system produces three types of recommendations. Required Approvals Based on change metadata, the system determines which roles should review the change. Example: | Domain | Suggested Approvers | | | | | Pricing | Finance + Product | | Billing | Engineering + Finance | | CRM | RevOps | These recommendations are derived from the organization's configured approval mappings. Evidence Requirements The system may recommend operational safeguards such as: • rollback procedures • monitoring plans • testing documentation • revenue validation checks These safeguards reduce the likelihood and impact of operational failures. Coordination Tasks Some changes require coordination across multiple teams. Examples: • notify finance of pricing updates • validate CRM field mappings • confirm billing rule compatibility Coordination Autopilot highlights these dependencies. Applying Recommendations Users can apply recommended governance steps with a single action. This dramatically reduces the time required to prepare a change for review. Why Coordination Matters Revenue systems are complex and interconnected. Changes to one system can affect multiple others. Coordination Autopilot helps teams ensure that: • stakeholders are aware of changes • dependencies are validated • safeguards are implemented

title: Notifications and Jobs
description: Understand how background jobs, notifications, and operational processing support the Solvren workflow.
content: Overview Solvren relies on background processing to keep operational workflows timely and visible. This typically includes notification dispatch, SLA evaluation, inbox generation, and digest generation. Why jobs matter Without reliable background processing, users may not receive approvals, reminders, or queue updates at the right time. Notification model Notifications may be delivered through in app surfaces, email, and Slack. Operational requirements Background jobs should be idempotent, observable, and retryable where appropriate. Why this matters to admins Admins and owners should understand that the user experience depends not only on visible UI flows, but also on healthy background job execution.

title: Architecture Overview
description: Platform architecture and key concepts.
content: Overview Solvren is built on Next.js with Supabase for data and auth. Key concepts include changes, approval workflows, evidence enforcement, and revenue impact reporting. Core Concepts Changes A change represents a revenue impacting modification (pricing, billing, integration, etc.). Each change has status, risk, and coordination metadata. Approval Workflow Approvals flow from approval roles and mappings. The system suggests approvers and tracks decisions. Evidence Evidence (screenshots, test results, docs) is required before submission. The platform enforces requirements by change type. Revenue Impact Report The Revenue Impact Report summarizes exposure, risk drivers, and mitigations. It powers executive views and SLA tracking. Tech Stack Frontend : Next.js 16, React 19 Backend : Next.js API routes, Supabase Auth : Supabase Auth Database : Supabase (PostgreSQL)

title: Revenue Impact Report
description: Understand how Revenue Impact Reports combine deterministic signals and structured analysis.
content: Purpose The Revenue Impact Report helps teams understand the financial and operational risk of a change before it ships. What it includes Typical report outputs include risk level, failure modes, impact areas, safeguards, and executive summary. Design model The report combines deterministic baseline signals, structured report generation, persisted report versions, and stale detection after material edits. Why versioning matters A report should reflect the current change state. If material fields change, the report may become stale and should be regenerated.

title: System Overview
description: High-level architecture of Solvren.
content: Overview Solvren is a governance platform designed to manage operational changes that impact revenue systems. The platform coordinates change workflows across multiple systems and teams. Core Components The system consists of several major components. Change Management Engine Tracks the lifecycle of operational changes. Handles: • intake workflows • lifecycle state transitions • coordination tracking Coordination Autopilot Analyzes change metadata and recommends: • required approvers • evidence requirements • operational safeguards Risk Analysis Engine Evaluates the potential financial impact of a change. Generates: • risk scores • failure mode analysis • mitigation recommendations Audit and Compliance Layer Tracks system actions and governance decisions. Provides a complete historical record of operational changes.

title: Screenshot Guidelines
description: Consistent screenshot patterns for Solvren documentation.
content: Overview Elite documentation uses consistent screenshot patterns. Follow these guidelines to maintain visual consistency across Solvren docs. Pattern 1 — Interface Overview Use for introducing a screen or feature. Structure: 1. Step explanation 2. Full UI screenshot 3. Caption explaining the interface Example MDX: Pattern 2 — Annotated UI Use when highlighting specific elements. Structure: 1. Numbered list describing each element 2. Annotated screenshot with callouts Example MDX: Pattern 3 — Workflow Screens Use for multi step flows. Structure: One screenshot per step Clear step headers Example MDX: Folder Structure Store screenshots in: Style Guidelines | Rule | Recommendation | | | | | Resolution | 1440px wide | | Format | PNG | | Theme | Use light mode | | Callouts | Simple numbered circles | | Cropping | Remove browser UI | Reference These patterns follow conventions used by Stripe, Vercel, and Supabase documentation.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
description: Common questions about Solvren.
content: What types of changes should be tracked? Solvren is designed for revenue impacting operational changes . Examples include: • pricing rule updates • billing configuration changes • CRM routing changes • revenue recognition updates Do all operational changes need approval? Not necessarily. Organizations can configure which domains require formal approvals. High risk domains such as billing or pricing typically require multiple reviewers. Can approvals be customized? Yes. Administrators can configure: • approval roles • approval mappings • domain specific requirements Can we integrate with Slack? Yes. Solvren can deliver notifications and approval alerts through Slack. Why is evidence required? Evidence ensures that operational safeguards exist before a change is implemented. This reduces operational risk and improves incident response readiness.

title: Create Your First Change
description: Walk through the complete Solvren workflow.
content: Overview This guide demonstrates the full lifecycle of a change in Solvren. By the end of this walkthrough you will have: • created a change • generated a coordination plan • analyzed revenue risk • submitted a change for approval Step 1 — Create the Change Draft Start by creating a new change. Provide the basic information: Title Description Systems involved Change type Domain Example: Title: Stripe pricing tier update Systems: Stripe, HubSpot Domain: Pricing Change type: Pricing configuration Step 2 — Complete Guided Intake Solvren collects structured information that determines coordination and risk. You will be asked to provide: Rollout method Customer impact Backfill requirements Implementation details Expected outcome This structured data allows the system to reason about potential failure modes. Step 3 — Generate the Coordination Plan Coordination Autopilot analyzes the change and generates: Suggested approvers Evidence requirements Operational safeguards Coordination tasks You can apply these suggestions with one click. This ensures governance requirements are applied consistently. Step 4 — Generate the Revenue Impact Report The Revenue Impact Report evaluates potential financial risk. The report includes: Risk score Failure modes Required safeguards Executive summary This report is used by reviewers to evaluate whether a change is safe to deploy. Step 5 — Provide Evidence Attach or complete the evidence required for the change. Examples include: Rollback plan Monitoring plan Test scenarios Revenue validation Evidence ensures the organization can safely recover if something goes wrong. Step 6 — Submit for Review Once readiness requirements are satisfied, submit the change. Reviewers will see: Change summary Revenue Impact Report Evidence checklist Coordination plan Timeline Approvers can then approve or request changes. Congratulations You have successfully completed the full Solvren change workflow.

title: Get Started
description: Learn how to set up Solvren and govern your first revenue-impacting change.
content: Welcome to Solvren Solvren is a governance platform for revenue impacting operational changes . Modern revenue systems span many tools—billing systems, CRM platforms, marketing automation, pricing logic, and reporting infrastructure. Changes to any of these systems can introduce financial risk if they are not coordinated correctly. Solvren helps teams: • Identify revenue risk before deployment • Coordinate cross functional approvals • Enforce operational safeguards • Document change decisions and evidence • Provide executive visibility into revenue risk How Solvren Works Solvren introduces a structured workflow for managing high impact operational changes. The workflow consists of five phases: 1️⃣ Draft the change Define what is being modified and which systems are involved. 2️⃣ Guided intake Capture structured information about rollout plans, customer impact, and operational dependencies. 3️⃣ Coordination Autopilot Solvren suggests approvers, evidence requirements, and coordination tasks. 4️⃣ Revenue Impact Analysis Generate a structured report identifying failure modes and risk exposure. 5️⃣ Approval and release Submit the change for cross functional review. Typical Changes Managed in Solvren Examples include: • Pricing or discount rule updates • Billing logic changes • CRM routing or lead assignment updates • Revenue recognition configuration changes • Contract structure changes • Payment processor configuration changes • Revenue reporting pipeline updates Steps to First Value If you're new to Solvren, follow this path: 1️⃣ Set up your organization Configure users, roles, and approval mappings. 2️⃣ Create your first change Walk through the intake workflow and generate a risk report. 3️⃣ Submit for review Experience the full approval workflow. Next Steps

title: Set Up Your Organization
description: Configure users, roles, and governance settings.
content: Overview Before teams can manage changes in Solvren, the organization must be configured. The setup process establishes: • user access • approval roles • governance policies • evidence enforcement rules This typically takes 10–15 minutes . Step 1 — Create Your Organization When you first sign in, you will be prompted to create an organization. Provide: • Organization name • Primary administrator • Default timezone Organizations are the security boundary for data, approvals, and policies. Step 2 — Invite Your Team Solvren works best when all stakeholders involved in revenue systems are included. Typical roles include: Engineering Finance Revenue Operations Product Analytics To invite users: 1. Open Settings → Users 2. Click Invite User 3. Enter email addresses 4. Assign a role Users will receive an email invitation. Step 3 — Configure User Roles Roles control what actions users can perform. Typical roles include: Owner Full system access. Can configure organization settings, roles, and governance policies. Admin Operational administrators responsible for maintaining governance configuration. Submitter Can create and submit changes. Reviewer Responsible for reviewing and approving changes. Step 4 — Configure Approval Roles Approval roles represent organizational responsibility , not individuals. Examples: Engineering Lead Finance Product Revenue Operations Security Users are assigned to these roles so the system can route approvals automatically. Step 5 — Configure Approval Mappings Approval mappings determine which roles must approve which changes . Mappings are based on: • change type • domain • systems involved Example: | Change Type | Required Approvers | | | | | Pricing change | Finance + Product | | Billing logic change | Engineering + Finance | | CRM routing change | RevOps | Solvren uses these mappings when generating Coordination Autopilot plans. Step 6 — Configure Evidence Enforcement Solvren ensures operational safeguards are documented. Examples of required evidence: Rollback plan Test scenarios Monitoring plan Revenue validation checks Evidence requirements can be configured by: • domain • system • change type Setup Complete Once setup is complete, your team can begin creating changes. Next: ➡

title: Approvals Workflow
description: How approval routing works in Solvren.
content: Overview Solvren ensures that revenue impacting changes receive appropriate oversight before implementation. Approval workflows ensure that: • relevant stakeholders review changes • cross team dependencies are addressed • governance policies are enforced Approval Roles Approvals are assigned based on roles , not individuals. Examples include: • Engineering Lead • Finance • Revenue Operations • Product Users are mapped to these roles in the organization settings. Approval Routing Approval routing is determined by the system based on: • change type • domain • systems involved For example: | Change | Approvers | | | | | Pricing configuration | Finance + Product | | Billing logic | Engineering + Finance | | CRM routing | RevOps | This mapping ensures the correct teams are always involved. Reviewer Actions Reviewers can take several actions. Approve Indicates that the reviewer agrees the change is safe to proceed. Request Changes Indicates issues must be addressed before approval. Reviewers may leave comments explaining their concerns. Approval Visibility Each change displays: • pending approvals • completed approvals • reviewer comments This allows teams to quickly understand the review status. Approval Best Practices Recommended practices include: • requiring multiple reviewers for high risk domains • including finance for pricing or billing changes • ensuring engineering reviews technical changes Proper approval configuration dramatically reduces operational risk.

title: Change Lifecycle
description: Understand the lifecycle of a change in Solvren.
content: Overview Every change created in Solvren moves through a defined lifecycle. This lifecycle ensures: • risks are identified • safeguards are documented • approvals are coordinated • changes are traceable Understanding this lifecycle helps teams operate the platform efficiently. Lifecycle Stages Changes move through the following states. Draft The change is being created and edited. At this stage users define: • title and description • systems involved • change type • domain • implementation details Draft changes are visible only to their authors and collaborators. Ready A change becomes Ready once all required information has been completed. Requirements may include: • structured intake fields • coordination plan generation • evidence checklist completion Ready changes can now be submitted for review. In Review Once submitted, the change enters In Review . During this stage: • approvers are notified • reviewers analyze risk reports • evidence is evaluated • comments may be added Reviewers can approve the change or request revisions. Approved When all required approvals are completed, the change becomes Approved . Approved changes indicate: • governance requirements were satisfied • reviewers accepted the risk assessment • safeguards are documented Approval does not automatically deploy the change , but signals readiness. Rejected Changes may be rejected if: • risk is too high • evidence is insufficient • implementation plans are unclear Rejected changes can be edited and resubmitted. Archived After completion, changes move to Archived . Archived changes provide a permanent record of: • decisions • evidence • approvals • operational context This historical record is valuable for audits and incident reviews. Lifecycle Summary | Stage | Purpose | | | | | Draft | Define change details | | Ready | Requirements satisfied | | In Review | Approvers evaluate risk | | Approved | Governance requirements met | | Rejected | Issues must be addressed | | Archived | Permanent record |

title: Change Workflow Guide
description: Follow the full lifecycle of a change from draft creation through approval and operational visibility.
content: Overview Solvren is built around a structured lifecycle for revenue impacting changes. A typical change moves through Draft, Guided Intake, Coordination Plan, Revenue Impact Report, Evidence Collection, In Review, and Approved or Rejected. Draft creation A submitter begins by creating a draft and entering title, description, systems, domain, and change type. Guided intake Guided intake captures rollout method, customer impact, backfill requirements, implementation context, and expected business outcome. Coordination and risk analysis Before submission, generate Coordination Autopilot and Revenue Impact Report. These artifacts ensure the right reviewers and safeguards are present. Evidence and readiness Collect required evidence such as rollback plan, test scenarios, monitoring plan, and validation evidence. Review and decision Reviewers evaluate change details, evidence completeness, Coordination Plan, Revenue Impact Report, and timeline history. After approval Approved changes remain visible in timelines, search, queues, and historical reporting for institutional memory.

title: Dashboard and Queues
description: Understand the operational dashboard, queue types, and how work is surfaced to different roles.
content: Dashboard purpose The dashboard gives users a live operational view of the work that matters most. Common cards and queues include My Approvals, In Review, Blocked, Overdue, and Recently Updated. My Approvals This queue shows changes requiring action from the current reviewer. Use it to prioritize direct responsibilities first. In Review This queue shows changes actively moving through the governance process. It helps teams monitor throughput and active operational exposure. Blocked Blocked changes are typically waiting on missing evidence, unresolved safeguards, or other required conditions. Overdue Overdue approvals show where review timing has slipped past expected SLA. This is often one of the most useful operational signals for admins and executives. Recently Updated This view helps users stay aware of current activity without opening every individual change. Visibility behavior Dashboard cards and queues reflect org role permissions, domain permissions, restricted visibility, and assigned reviewer logic where applicable. Recommended usage by role Submitter: Focus on drafts, in review owned changes, and blocked items you need to unblock. Reviewer: Focus on My Approvals, blocked by evidence changes you are reviewing, and overdue items. Admin / Owner: Focus on org wide operational health, overdue work, blocked work, and review bottlenecks. Executive: Focus on high risk changes, blocked and overdue items, and broader risk visibility.

title: Evidence Requirements
description: How operational safeguards are documented in Solvren.
content: Overview Evidence ensures that operational safeguards are documented before a change is implemented. These safeguards reduce the impact of failures and enable rapid recovery. Types of Evidence Typical evidence includes: Rollback Plan Describes how the change can be reversed if issues occur. Example: • revert configuration • restore previous pricing • disable new routing rules Test Scenarios Describe how the change was validated before release. Examples: • billing simulations • pricing validation tests • data integrity checks Monitoring Plan Describes how the system will detect problems after deployment. Examples: • revenue anomaly alerts • payment failure monitoring • billing reconciliation checks Revenue Validation Ensures revenue reporting remains correct. Examples: • sample invoice verification • accounting reconciliation • reporting validation Evidence Enforcement Organizations may configure required evidence types based on: • change type • system • domain Changes cannot be submitted until all required evidence is provided. Why Evidence Matters Operational failures often occur when safeguards are missing. Evidence ensures that teams have: • tested the change • planned recovery procedures • validated revenue impact

title: Governance Best Practices
description: Recommended ways to structure operational governance in Solvren.
content: Overview Effective governance requires thoughtful configuration of approvals and safeguards. The following best practices help organizations minimize operational risk. Require Finance Review for Pricing Changes Pricing updates can directly affect revenue and customer billing. Recommended reviewers: • Finance • Product Require Engineering Review for System Changes Changes that modify system logic should include technical review. Recommended reviewers: • Engineering • Platform team Enforce Evidence for High Risk Domains High risk domains should require operational safeguards. Examples: | Domain | Required Evidence | | | | | Billing | rollback plan | | Pricing | validation tests | | Revenue recognition | financial reconciliation | Encourage Cross Team Coordination Operational changes often affect multiple departments. Include reviewers from: • product • engineering • finance • revenue operations Review Historical Changes Audit history provides valuable insights. Regularly review: • rejected changes • incident causing changes • frequently modified systems This helps refine governance policies over time.

title: Notifications
description: How Solvren keeps teams informed about change activity.
content: Overview Solvren notifies users when action is required. Notifications ensure that approvals and coordination tasks are not missed. Notification Types Users may receive notifications for: • approval requests • missing evidence • reviewer comments • completed approvals • overdue changes Notification Channels Notifications may be delivered through: Email Email alerts notify users when they must review or approve changes. Slack Slack notifications provide real time updates within team collaboration channels. In App Alerts In app notifications appear within the Solvren interface. These alerts provide quick visibility when users log in. Managing Notifications Users can configure notification preferences in their account settings. Administrators may configure organization wide defaults. Best Practices Teams should ensure: • reviewers receive approval alerts • operational teams receive deployment alerts • high risk domains trigger immediate notifications

title: Reviewer Guide
description: Learn how reviewers evaluate changes, verify safeguards, and make approval decisions confidently.
content: Reviewer responsibilities Reviewers evaluate whether a change is safe, well supported, and ready. They must understand why the change exists, what systems it touches, what risk it creates, what safeguards are required, and whether evidence is complete. Start from My Approvals Use the My Approvals queue to surface changes that require your action. What to review first Review in order: change summary, systems and domain, Revenue Impact Report, Coordination Plan rationale, evidence checklist, timeline. Reading the Revenue Impact Report The report helps reviewers understand risk level, failure modes, impact areas, and required safeguards. Reading the Coordination Plan The plan answers why you were included, who else was suggested, what evidence was expected, and what blockers exist. Evidence enforcement If required evidence is missing, approval may be blocked. Typical issues: rollback plan missing, monitoring plan not provided, validation incomplete. Reviewers should not treat approval as a substitute for missing evidence. Evidence and approval reinforce each other. Approve or reject Approve when the change is understood, evidence is sufficient, safeguards are appropriate, and risk is acceptable. Reject when evidence is incomplete, the change is poorly scoped, or safeguards are inadequate.

title: Search and Notifications
description: Learn how to find the right records quickly and stay aware of changes that require attention.
content: Search Solvren search helps users quickly find changes, systems, approvals, evidence, and users. What to search by Useful search terms include change title, system name, domain, evidence type, and user name or email. Notifications Notifications surface workflow events that require attention. Examples: approval requested, evidence required, approval overdue, change approved or rejected, comment or update notifications if configured. In app notifications Use the in app notification surface to track product activity without leaving the application. Email and Slack routing If configured, notifications can also be delivered by email or Slack. Best practice Use search for retrieval and notifications for awareness. Search helps you find what exists. Notifications help you know what needs action.

title: Search and Audit History
description: How to search changes and review historical decisions.
content: Overview Solvren provides powerful search and audit capabilities. These features allow teams to quickly locate historical decisions and investigate past changes. Search Users can search across: • change titles • systems • domains • reviewers Search helps teams answer questions such as: • "Who approved the pricing change last quarter?" • "When was billing logic modified?" Audit History Every action in the system is recorded. Tracked actions include: • change creation • evidence updates • approval decisions • configuration changes Why Audit Logs Matter Audit logs support: • operational accountability • compliance requirements • incident investigations Organizations can trace exactly who approved or modified a change.

title: Troubleshooting
description: Common issues and how to resolve them.
content: Change Cannot Be Submitted This usually occurs when required information is missing. Check: • evidence checklist completion • required fields in intake • approval mappings Missing Approvers If no approvers appear, verify: • approval role configuration • user role assignments • approval mapping settings Administrators can configure these in the organization settings. Notifications Not Received Check the following: • user notification preferences • Slack integration configuration • organization email settings Evidence Not Accepted Some organizations require specific evidence types. Ensure that required evidence items are completed before submitting the change. Unable to Find Historical Changes Use search filters such as: • domain • system • reviewer Search supports full text queries across change titles and descriptions.

title: User Guide
description: Complete guide to working with Solvren.
content: Overview Solvren provides a structured process for governing revenue impacting changes. This guide explains how operators interact with the system during normal workflows. Dashboard The dashboard provides visibility into: Changes awaiting approval Changes assigned to you Blocked or overdue changes Operational alerts Queues help teams prioritize reviews and identify bottlenecks. Creating Changes Changes represent operational modifications that could impact revenue. Examples include: Pricing updates Billing configuration changes Lead routing changes Revenue recognition adjustments Each change follows a structured workflow to ensure coordination. Change Lifecycle Every change moves through the following states: Draft Ready In Review Approved Rejected Archived These states provide visibility into operational readiness. Reviewing Changes Reviewers evaluate: Risk analysis Evidence completeness Operational safeguards Implementation plan Reviewers can: Approve Request changes Add comments Evidence Enforcement Solvren ensures critical safeguards are documented. Evidence may include: Rollback procedures Testing documentation Monitoring plans Revenue validation strategies Changes cannot be submitted until required evidence is provided. Notifications Solvren notifies users when: Changes require review Evidence is missing Approvals are completed Changes become overdue Notifications can be delivered via: Email Slack In app alerts Search Solvren supports full text search across: Changes Systems Domains Approvers This allows teams to quickly locate historical decisions. Audit History Every action is recorded in the audit log. Tracked events include: Change creation Evidence updates Approval decisions Configuration changes Audit history ensures accountability and traceability.

title: 5-Minute Jira Setup
description: Connect Jira to Solvren in about 5 minutes.
content: Overview This guide walks you through connecting Jira Cloud to Solvren in about 5 minutes. No developer help required—admins can complete setup from org settings. Step 1 — Connect Jira 1. Go to Org settings → Integrations → Jira → Setup 2. Click Connect Jira 3. Solvren will request permission to: Read Jira issues and projects Sync governance status Manage webhooks for real time updates 4. Authorize the app in Atlassian 5. You'll return to Solvren with Jira connected Step 2 — Select Projects Choose the Jira projects you want Solvren to monitor. Typical projects: Platform Engineering Revenue Systems Internal Tools Billing or Payments Select only projects where changes might affect revenue, billing, or financial systems. You can add more later. Step 3 — Map Statuses Map Jira statuses to Solvren governance states so status changes sync correctly. Example mapping: | Jira Status | Solvren State | | | | | Open | Draft | | In Progress | Ready | | Ready for Review | Ready | | In Review | In Review | | Blocked | Blocked | | Done | Approved | | Rejected | Rejected | Customize mappings to match your workflow. Solvren uses these to keep governance status in sync when Jira issues change. Step 4 — Enable Features Recommended feature toggles: Webhook Sync — Real time status updates when Jira issues change (recommended) Issue Property Sync — Show governance status in Jira (status, risk score, approval progress) Comment Sync — Post governance events as Jira comments Enable all three for the best experience. Webhook Sync requires webhook registration; Solvren handles this automatically. Step 5 — Validate Click Test Connection . Solvren checks: Jira authentication API access Project permissions Webhook registration (if enabled) If any check fails, follow the on screen guidance. Common issues: Webhook not registered — Ensure the Jira admin has approved webhooks Project access — Verify your Jira user has access to selected projects Step 6 — Done After validation passes, Solvren will: Detect Jira status changes Sync governance data with linked issues Mirror approval decisions in Jira Display governance status in Jira (when Issue Property Sync is enabled) You can return to Jira integration settings anytime to add projects, adjust status mappings, or change feature toggles. Screenshots The setup wizard includes: 1. OAuth screen — Atlassian authorization 2. Project selector — Choose projects to monitor 3. Status mapping table — Map Jira → Solvren statuses 4. Feature toggle panel — Webhook, property, and comment sync 5. Validation results — Connection health checks 6. Success confirmation — Setup complete Related — Full integration reference, webhook setup, API details — How changes flow through Solvren — Initial org configuration

title: Governance Playbook
description: Recommended governance structure for revenue operations.
content: Overview Governance policies determine how organizations review and approve operational changes. This playbook provides recommended governance structures for revenue operations teams. Recommended Approval Structure Organizations should include stakeholders responsible for financial accuracy and system implementation. Example approval structure: | Domain | Required Approvers | | | | | Pricing | Finance + Product | | Billing | Engineering + Finance | | CRM | Revenue Operations | This ensures that both business and technical stakeholders review changes. High Risk Domains Certain domains require stronger governance safeguards. Examples include: • billing configuration • pricing updates • revenue recognition logic For these domains, organizations should require: • multiple reviewers • evidence documentation • validation testing Evidence Policies Governance policies should enforce operational safeguards. Recommended evidence types: • rollback procedures • monitoring plans • validation tests These safeguards reduce operational risk. Continuous Improvement Organizations should periodically review governance policies. Recommended practices include: • reviewing rejected changes • analyzing incident causing changes • refining approval requirements Governance policies should evolve as systems grow more complex.

title: Operational Maturity Model
description: Evaluate the maturity of revenue operations governance.
content: Overview Organizations vary widely in how they manage operational changes. Solvren supports teams at different stages of operational maturity. Level 1 — Ad Hoc Changes are implemented without structured governance. Characteristics: • informal approvals • minimal documentation • reactive incident management Risk level: High Level 2 — Documented Organizations begin documenting changes. Characteristics: • written change descriptions • some approval processes • limited safeguards Risk level: Moderate Level 3 — Governed Organizations implement structured governance workflows. Characteristics: • formal approval policies • evidence requirements • cross team coordination Risk level: Low Level 4 — Automated Governance Governance workflows are automated and integrated into operational systems. Characteristics: • automated risk analysis • automated approval routing • structured audit history Risk level: Very Low Level 5 — Predictive Operations Advanced organizations use predictive systems to prevent operational failures. Characteristics: • predictive risk analysis • automated safeguards • continuous operational monitoring Risk level: Minimal

title: Changelog
description: Detailed list of product changes.
content: Version 1.0 Initial platform release. Added • Change lifecycle tracking • Risk analysis engine • Coordination Autopilot • Evidence enforcement system • Approval workflows • Notification system Infrastructure • secure organization isolation • audit logging • role based access control Future entries will track all changes to the platform.

title: Release Notes
description: Track new features and improvements in Solvren.
content: Overview The Release Notes provide a history of product improvements and new features. Each release documents: • new capabilities • performance improvements • bug fixes • security updates Version 1.0 Initial public release of Solvren. Core Features • Structured change intake workflows • Coordination Autopilot recommendations • Revenue Impact Report generation • Approval routing system • Evidence enforcement • Audit history and search Platform Capabilities • role based access control • organization governance settings • Slack and email notifications • operational change lifecycle tracking Future releases will continue to expand automation and governance capabilities.

title: Change Review Runbook
description: Step-by-step guide for reviewing operational changes.
content: Overview This runbook provides guidance for reviewers evaluating operational changes. Following a consistent review process improves governance quality. Step 1 — Review Change Summary Review the change description to understand: • what is being modified • which systems are affected • expected outcomes Ensure the change description clearly explains the purpose. Step 2 — Evaluate the Risk Report Review the Revenue Impact Report. Check: • risk score • identified failure modes • recommended safeguards Ensure potential risks are clearly understood. Step 3 — Review Evidence Verify that operational safeguards are documented. Check for: • rollback procedures • testing documentation • monitoring plans Ensure the organization can detect and recover from failures. Step 4 — Evaluate Dependencies Review the systems involved in the change. Determine whether additional stakeholders should be consulted. Examples: • finance review for billing changes • engineering review for system logic changes Step 5 — Approve or Request Changes If safeguards are sufficient, approve the change. If issues remain, request additional evidence or clarification. Key Principle Reviewers should ensure that every change answers two questions: What could go wrong? How will we detect and recover if it does?

title: Troubleshooting Playbook
description: Operational troubleshooting guidance.
content: Change Stuck in Review Possible causes: • missing approvals • incomplete evidence • reviewer unavailable Resolution steps: 1. Check approval status 2. Verify evidence checklist 3. notify pending reviewers Missing Evidence If evidence requirements are not satisfied: 1. review required evidence types 2. upload missing documentation 3. update change submission Incorrect Approval Routing If incorrect reviewers appear: 1. verify approval role mappings 2. check domain configuration 3. update governance policies

title: Auditability
description: See how Solvren preserves a clear historical record of change activity, approvals, evidence, and coordination.
content: Why auditability matters Solvren preserves the operational story of a change. That includes when it was created, when it moved into review, when evidence was added, who approved or rejected it, and when coordination or risk artifacts were generated. Timeline and audit history Each change has a timeline that records major actions in order. Typical events include change created, change submitted, evidence provided, coordination plan generated, revenue impact report generated, approval approved or rejected. What auditability supports A strong audit trail helps with incident analysis, governance reviews, operational handoffs, executive reporting, and compliance conversations. A good governance system should explain not just the final decision, but how the organization arrived there.

title: Evidence Enforcement
description: Understand how required evidence blocks approval and ensures safeguards are actually completed.
content: Why evidence enforcement exists Approvals without evidence often create the illusion of governance without the substance of real operational safety. Solvren uses evidence enforcement so required safeguards are not optional in practice. Common evidence examples Examples include rollback plan, test plan, monitoring plan, revenue validation, and reconciliation proof. How enforcement works A change may be blocked from approval if required evidence is still missing. This ensures reviewers are not forced to approve blind, submitters know what must be complete, and the governance process is consistent. Recommended use Use required evidence for safeguards that are truly necessary, and recommended evidence for items that improve review quality without blocking progress.

title: Security Overview
description: How Solvren protects organizational data.
content: Overview Solvren is designed with security as a foundational principle. The platform ensures that sensitive operational and financial data is protected through multiple layers of security controls. Access Control User access is governed through: • authentication • role based permissions • organization isolation Users only access data within their organization. Data Protection All data is encrypted: • in transit using HTTPS • at rest using secure storage mechanisms Sensitive operational data is never exposed publicly. Auditability All critical actions are logged to provide a complete audit trail. This enables organizations to maintain operational accountability. Secure Architecture Solvren uses modern infrastructure practices to maintain system security and reliability. Security practices include: • secure deployment pipelines • regular monitoring • controlled administrative access

title: RBAC
description: Learn how role-based access control works across changes, approvals, settings, and operational views.
content: Overview Solvren uses role based access control to determine what users can do in the platform. Roles define capabilities such as creating changes, submitting changes, approving changes, managing users, and managing governance settings. Why RBAC exists RBAC ensures users only perform actions they are authorized to perform, administrative configuration stays controlled, and review authority is intentional. Example capability boundaries Submitters can create and submit changes Reviewers can approve assigned work Admins can manage governance configuration Viewers have read only access where allowed Relationship to other access controls RBAC works together with domain permissions, restricted visibility, and explicit access grants. RBAC answers what a user can do in general. Domain permissions and restricted visibility answer what specific changes they can see or review.

title: Restricted Visibility
description: Understand how Solvren protects sensitive changes using restricted access and explicit grants.
content: What restricted visibility means Some changes are too sensitive for broad org visibility. Examples include security sensitive billing changes and confidential legal changes. For these cases, Solvren supports restricted visibility. Who can typically access restricted changes Depending on policy, access may be limited to owners, admins, the change creator, assigned reviewers, and explicitly granted users. Why this matters Restricted visibility prevents sensitive work from leaking through queues, search, timeline activity, result counts, and notifications. Best practice Use restricted visibility intentionally for high sensitivity workflows, but do not overuse it for normal operational changes.

title: Seeded Personas and Data
description: Understand the seeded users, roles, systems, and sample changes used for UAT and demos.
content: Why seeded data matters Seeded data makes testing and demos repeatable. It ensures every tester starts from the same baseline and role behavior can be validated consistently. Seeded personas Typical personas include Owner, Admin, Submitter, Reviewer, Viewer, Finance Reviewer, Security Reviewer, Restricted Reviewer. Seeded sample changes Typical seeded records include high risk pricing change, blocked billing logic change, approved revenue recognition change, restricted security change, overdue approval change, draft in progress. How to use this data Use the seeded personas and sample changes as the basis for UAT scripts, E2E tests, demos, and screenshot generation.

title: UAT Test Scripts
description: Run structured user acceptance tests against the full Solvren workflow.
content: UAT goals User Acceptance Testing should confirm that the product works for real users in realistic workflows. Core test groups Recommended UAT coverage includes owner and admin setup, submitter workflow, reviewer workflow, evidence enforcement, restricted visibility, search and queues, notifications, dashboard correctness. Typical UAT structure Each test should include persona, prerequisites, steps, expected outcomes, pass/fail result, and notes. Example scenario: Submitter creates and submits a high risk change 1. Log in as the submitter 2. Create a draft 3. Complete guided intake 4. Generate Coordination Plan 5. Generate Revenue Impact Report 6. Apply approvers and evidence 7. Submit for review Expected outcomes: readiness becomes satisfied, report and plan appear, review status updates, approval request becomes visible to the assigned reviewer. Use seeded personas and sample changes for UAT so every run is comparable and easier to debug.
